dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, I've been having shortness of breath for about 30 minutes.
Can you tell me when the breathing issues started and if anything helped before we arrived?
Yes, I tried the nebulizer three times, but it only gave a little relief.
Understood. Are you having any pain or other symptoms along with the shortness of breath?
Just to check, can you tell me your full name, the date, and where you are?
Angel, Friday, 4th of January, and I'm in Richmond.
Thank you. That shows you're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Ma'am, I've taken your initial vitals. Your pulse is 95, respirations are 24, and your oxygen saturation is 87%.
We're going to help with your breathing. We'll begin a nebulizer treatment and put oxygen on.
Ma'am, your airway is patent and clear. We'll maintain it in your current seated position.
Your breathing is labored, breath sounds are clear but diminished bilaterally, and your oxygen saturation has improved to 96%.
Your pulse is now 98, and your blood pressure is 128 over 100 after starting oxygen and the nebulizer.
Based on observations, your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15; you're alert, oriented, and interaction is normal.
Angel, when you were in the hospital, were they treating you for anything in particular?
Yes, they diagnosed me with MRSA and had me in the hospital for about two weeks.
Were you given anything at the hospital that helped you breathe?
I've had asthma since I was a child, although it is only moderate. I have a nebulizer at home with Albuterol and Atrovent.
I see. You shared you're also on a few other medications.
Yes, Lantus, Coreg, Aspirin, Lipitor, Celexa, Catapres, and Flexeril.
And any other prescriptions or over-the-counter meds?
Yeah, NovoLog, Prilosec, Zofran, Prednisone, and Vancomycin.
Ma'am, we're monitoring your blood pressure now.
Your initial blood pressure was 0, but now it has improved to 128/100.
Angel, we're going to check your blood glucose now.
Your first reading was not available, but shortly after the nebulizer, it came to 290.
We'll also be placing a 4-lead ECG on you to monitor your heart continuously.
Angel, a 12-lead ECG has been completed and shows sinus rhythm with a short PR interval.
No signs of arrhythmias were detected on the ECG per the field impression.
Angel, can you describe any events that led up to your calling 911 tonight?
My shortness of breath got worse as the night wore on. I finally couldn't ignore it.
Did anything in particular seem to trigger it, or does anything ease it?
I don't think anything eased it, and I can't pinpoint a trigger. It was gradual.
Reviewing your vitals from the beginning, your core stats have improved on treatment.
You received two full Duoneb treatments with Albuterol and Atrovent via oxygen at 8 liters per minute.
That dosing per your standing orders, as you have a PICC line and we don't want to place an IV.
After those treatments, your oxygen was reduced to 6 liters per nasal cannula, and your saturation stayed at or above 99%.
Angel, you mentioned a few other conditions on your medical chart - any bring up allergies here?
Ibuprofen, Darvocet-N, Toradol, tramadol, Morphine.
Understood and noted in the chart so the hospital team is aware.
You mentioned walking with slight weakness too, is that new or coming from your bipolar diagnosis?
Yeah, I have bipolar disorder, major depression, and anxiety. It's something I manage long term.
Also, we've noted an afebrile status and A.O.x4 status in your chart as part of our assessment.
And you also have hyperlipidemia and hypertension?
Yes, I take Coreg, and other meds like Hydrochlorothiazide and Losartan.
What about diabetes - you take Lantus and NovoLog for that?
Yes, and if I'm not careful, my sugars can go high like they did today.
You're also taking Norco and Clonidine for hypertension and muscle relaxation, with Atorvastatin and Cyclobenzaprine for your cholesterol and muscle tension.
We have added these medications to the record as active prescriptions.
And Alprazolam, for your anxiety, and Citalopram for depression, yes?
Correct. And Ferrous Sulfate to manage some prior iron deficiency.
During the call, your blood sugar peaked at 290. We monitored it as that is another risk for respiratory distress.
No rash, bruising, or jaundice. Skin is warm and dry.
Pupils are reactive and equal. No signs of neck rigidity or deformity.
No drainage or obstruction detected in the ears or nose.
Breath sounds are diminished in all fields, but no asymmetry or significant work of breathing noted.
Your abdomen is soft and not tender. No signs of guarding or distension.
No deformities or swelling in your extremities observed.
No reported urgency or changes in urination or normal stool appearance.
Angel, we are securing you now for transport in a semi-Fowler's position.
With the current vitals, pulse 84, BP 140/82, oxygen saturation 100%, and you are still afebrile.
Angel, during your stay in the hospital, is your PICC line still in your arm?
Yes, it's in my left upper arm, so I can't get an IV again unless they take it out.
We've noted that in the chart. We'll secure your arms to prevent moving the line.
We are transporting you to St. Mary's ED where you will be handed off to the medical team.
We've recorded your vitals, interventions, and medical history for the hospital team.
No changes in condition noted during transport, and your vitals stayed steady.
We maintained a steady pace for safety. You showed improvement throughout the ride.
Your team will be met with vitals in the normal range and no signs of adverse effects of treatment.
You're fully secured in transport with straps, and we've monitored you for the entire trip.
We've maintained a position that seems comfortable for you, and your breathing is stable.
Your relative is here and ready to walk with us to the hospital's emergency room.
The stretcher is at a comfortable and stable temperature for the duration of transit.
If you feel anything different in your breathing, please let me know immediately.
We used the semi-Fowler's as that's your most comfortable upright resting posture.
And we completed your nebulizer treatments in that position for consistent breathing support.
All straps are tight and we will minimize your movement on the way.
St. Mary's is the best choice for continued treatment and has your records from your previous stay.
The revised care plan matches the treatment given and we handed over about 40 minutes into transport.
The conditions during your travel were temperate as needed, and no delays were necessary.
Final vitals at 84 BPM, 140/82 BP, 18 breaths, and 100% saturation recorded.
All treatments most stabilizing and all medications administered were noted during the drive.
We reported all medications on your chart, including prednisone and your other diabetes meds.
No complications with your transport and you appear better from initial distress.
Expect a transport free of emergency escalation and vitals all back in normal ranges.
Your care plan going forward seems coachable by the staff and will include follow-up instructions.
Let me know if there is anything I or Alex need to clarify for your care team on arrival.
We'll continue our support and provide the hospital team with any further details they need.
We'll remain available for any follow-up calls, and all meds used are ready for handover.
The call ended at 04:24 AM, and the patient was handed over safely without any further complications.
Total transport time taken was about 1 hour, and Angel was stable the entire time.
No complications or changes occurred during the journey to the facility.
Thanks to the effective nebulizer therapy and monitoring, your vitals remained steady.
We engaged cold transport measures due to the stability of your condition and improvement throughout the ride.
You've done great, and I appreciate you being patient through the care process tonight.
I hope your visit ends well, and your breathing issues are resolved effectively.
Do reach out to your care team and ask for those follow-up checkups once out of the hospital.
Health is important, so continue your medication and rest.
We will walk you through the transfer process now. One of us will stay with you all the way.
All protocol steps followed, no deviations from medical respiratory distress protocol 3-11.
